title: Galette de sarrasin
subtitle: Buckwheat crêpes with ham, egg, and Gruyère
excerpt: Traditional Breton buckwheat crêpes, thicker and more substantial than wheat flour ones, with a slightly nutty and earthy flavour. Filled with the classic combination of ham, a fried egg, and melted Gruyère (the complète) and folded around the sides so the egg is exposed in the centre.
sourceNotes: Galettes de sarrasin are documented in Breton cookery literature from at least the eighteenth century. Buckwheat (sarrasin in French, blé noir in Breton dialect) arrived in Brittany from Central Asia in the fifteenth century and became a staple. The crêperie tradition and the complète filling are standard throughout contemporary Brittany.

recipe.batchNotes: Make a full batch of batter and keep it in the fridge for up to 2 days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter needs at least 1 hour to rest; overnight resting produces a better result.

content.text: Buckwheat flour makes a batter with no gluten, which means the crêpes are more fragile than wheat ones but also crisper at the edges. The batter is thin and spreads easily; the trick is heat. A galette cooked over insufficiently hot heat comes out pale and bendy. It should go into the pan over medium-high heat and come out with lacy, slightly crisped edges after about 2 minutes.

The batter needs at least an hour to rest in the fridge, which allows the flour to fully absorb the liquid and produces a smoother batter. The first galette is almost always a test — the pan's temperature and the batter's consistency both need calibrating. Adjust the heat and the batter consistency (add a tablespoon of water if it seems too thick) before cooking the rest.

items.prepNote: ham or good cooked ham, thinly sliced

content.text: Whisk together the {{buckwheat-flour}} of buckwheat flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and add the {{eggs}} eggs. Whisk the eggs into the flour from the centre outward. Gradually add the {{whole-milk}} of milk and {{water}} of cold water, whisking constantly until the batter is smooth and the consistency of single cream. Stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter. Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour, or overnight.

Heat a 26 cm frying pan or crêpe pan over medium-high heat. Rub the surface with a little butter. Pour in enough batter to just coat the pan (about 80 ml), swirling immediately to spread it thinly. Cook for 1 to 2 minutes until the edges look dry and lightly crisped. Add a small amount of {{gruyere}} of grated Gruyère and a few slices of {{back-bacon}} of ham to one half of the galette, then crack a filling {{eggs}} egg directly onto the other half. Cook until the egg white is just set, about 2 minutes. Fold the four edges of the galette inward around the filling, leaving the yolk exposed in the centre. Slide onto a warm plate.

items.cause: Batter too thick, or galette overcooked and brittle
items.fix: Thin the batter with a tablespoon of cold water; cook until just set but not crisped all the way through — a folded galette should bend without cracking

items.symptom: Batter is spreading unevenly with thick patches
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or batter added too slowly
items.fix: Pour the batter in one motion and swirl the pan immediately before the first contact point sets

items.symptom: Egg white is still raw when the galette is done
items.cause: Heat too low, or lid not used
items.fix: Cover the pan with a lid for 1 minute once the egg is in — steam cooks the white without overcooking the yolk

content.text: The galette de sarrasin is the food of Brittany in the way that pasta is the food of Italy: a staple built from what grows there, turned into something particular and regional. Buckwheat tolerates the wet, acidic soils of the Breton interior where wheat does not. The crêperie is the social institution that built up around it: a place to eat galettes and sweet crêpes, usually both, in a particular order. The complète (ham, egg, and cheese) is the reference filling, the one against which everything else is measured.
